_meta.purpose: Per-state electrical data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages
_meta.sources: NFPA NEC adoption tracker (state code-cycle adoption); state electrical licensing boards (verified URLs); BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 47-2111 Electricians state means); USDA-NRCS frost-line maps for buried-conduit depth; NOAA/Vaisala National Lightning Detection Network flash-density tiers.

fields.iecc_zone: 2021 IECC climate zone (1-8, A=moist, B=dry, C=marine)
fields.nec_code_year: Currently adopted edition of the National Electrical Code (NFPA 70)
fields.nec_amendment_summary: Brief summary of state-specific NEC amendments or local-code overlays
fields.license_status: required (state-issued license + exam) / registration / municipal-only / none
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for electrical contractor licensing in this state
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential panel upgrade or service-line work
fields.electrician_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2111 Electricians (USD)
fields.service_size_typical: Typical residential service entrance size for new construction
fields.avg_panel_upgrade_low: Typical low end of a 200A panel upgrade with new meter base (USD)
fields.avg_panel_upgrade_high: Typical high end (200A panel + service-line + grounding + permit) (USD)
fields.lightning_tier: very-high / high / moderate / low — NOAA Vaisala 10-year flash density
fields.conduit_depth_class: deep / moderate / shallow — buried-conduit design depth class
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific electrical code, license, or service rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary environmental driver of electrical service design in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.nec_amendment_summary: Alabama adopts NEC by reference under Alabama Electrical Contractor Act §34-36-14, with statewide enforcement through the Alabama Electrical Contractors Board
AL.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama §34-36-1 splits the Electrical Contractors Board from the Home Builders Licensure Board — Master Electrician licensure is governed exclusively by the AECB under §34-36-7, with three tiers (Apprentice, Journeyman, Master) and a separate Electrical Contractor business license required to advertise or hold residential service contracts.
AL.climate_concern: The Mobile-to-Tuscaloosa belt sits inside the highest cloud-to-ground lightning flash density in the United States outside Florida — service-entrance surge protection (Type 1 SPD at the meter or Type 2 panel-mounted) is now standard on coastal and Black Belt panel upgrades, and grounding electrode resistance under the Alabama Electrical Code is verified to ≤25 ohms with a single rod or supplemented by a second rod six feet away.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.nec_amendment_summary: Alaska adopts NEC under 8 AAC 70 with arctic-construction amendments — frost-protected service conduit and Arctic-pipe utilidor distribution are mandatory in unsewered villages
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Labor — Mechanical Inspection Section (Electrical Administrator certificate)
AK.permit_typical: borough electrical permit usually required for panel upgrade and any new service
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska Statute 18.62.075 requires every electrical worker — apprentice through journeyman — to hold a state-issued Certificate of Fitness, a credential entirely distinct from the Master/Administrator licenses used in the Lower 48; and Anchorage, Fairbanks, and Mat-Su Borough additionally require frost-protected shallow-foundation grounding documentation under their borough mechanical codes.
AK.climate_concern: Continuous and discontinuous permafrost across the Interior forces service conduit to either be insulated above-grade or heat-traced — burying an aluminum SE-cable to the Lower-48 default of 24 inches simply locks the conductor inside seasonally-thawing active layer that shears the conduit annually, so Fairbanks code rejects below-grade service in favor of above-ground meter pedestals on adfreeze-rated piles.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.nec_amendment_summary: Arizona has no statewide electrical code adoption — Phoenix uses 2017 NEC, Tucson uses 2017 NEC with local amendments, while Pima County and rural areas may operate on older cycles
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license C-11 (Electrical) or CR-11 (residential)
AZ.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade, EV charging, and PV interconnection
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona has no statewide electrical code adoption — the Industrial Commission of Arizona enforces NFPA 70 only on state-jurisdictional projects, and each municipality adopts its own NEC cycle (Phoenix on 2017, Tempe on 2020, Pima County on 2014 in unincorporated areas), forcing solar and EV-charging contractors to maintain three or four parallel install standards within a single ROC C-11 service area.
AZ.climate_concern: Phoenix attic and outdoor disconnect ambients exceed 150°F during summer afternoons, derating THWN-2 conductors and forcing copper rather than aluminum on long PV homerun pulls — and Sonoran monsoon haboobs deposit silicate dust into outdoor meter sockets and panel knockouts annually, requiring NEMA 3R-rated combiner boxes and gasketed in-use covers on every exterior receptacle.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.nec_amendment_summary: Arkansas adopts NEC under Ark. Code §17-28 with Department of Labor & Licensing enforcement, and the Department waives local amendments for cities under 60,000 population without a chartered inspection program
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Department of Labor & Licensing — Board of Electrical Examiners
AR.permit_typical: state electrical permit issued by Department of Labor inspector outside chartered cities
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas places electrical licensure under the state Department of Labor rather than a contractor licensing board — Ark. Code §17-28-101 makes residential electrical work a labor-regulated trade, and the Board of Electrical Examiners directly issues permits and inspects work in any unincorporated area or municipality without a chartered inspection program, with Master, Journeyman, Residential Master, and Residential Journeyman as the four separately credentialed tiers.
AR.climate_concern: The Boston Mountain and Ouachita National Forest corridors face summer cumulonimbus development with cloud-to-ground lightning rates above eight strikes per square mile per year — service-entrance surge protection at the panel is increasingly written into Arkansas Department of Labor inspection lists, and rural Ozark service drops are routinely upgraded from #2 aluminum SE-cable to copper service-entrance conductors after repeated near-strike damage.

CA.name: California
CA.nec_amendment_summary: California Electrical Code (CCR Title 24 Part 3, 2022 edition) is based on 2020 NEC with Title 24 Part 6 energy and PV interconnection amendments — solar-ready, EV-ready, and battery-ready conduit are mandatory on new residential construction
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-10 (Electrical)
CA.permit_typical: city electrical permit required; Title 24 + CALGreen efficiency and solar-readiness verification on new fixtures
CA.distinctive_law: California is the only state where every new single-family home built since the 2019 Title 24 cycle must include photovoltaic generation sized to annual electricity demand under CCR Title 24 Part 6 §150.1(c)14 — and Senate Bill 49 plus Title 24 §150.0(o) layer EV-ready and battery-ready raceway requirements onto every panel upgrade, so CSLB C-10 contractors routinely upsize a 100A service to 200A specifically to meet PV + EVSE + battery demand factors regardless of immediate equipment install.
CA.climate_concern: Pacific Gas & Electric and Southern California Edison Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) events across the Hayward, San Andreas, and Diablo fault zones have made whole-house transfer switches and battery backup interconnection a standard line item on coastal and foothill panel upgrades — the 2022 CEC requires bonding and grounding of any battery storage system to the main service ground, with seismic flexible conduit at every motor or load-center connection in mapped liquefaction zones.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.nec_amendment_summary: Colorado SB 13-126 prohibits unlicensed electricians from pulling permits — DORA State Electrical Board adopts current NEC cycle with statewide enforcement and Front Range altitude-derating advisories
CO.license_board_name: Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies — State Electrical Board (Master/Journeyman/Residential tiers)
CO.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required; rural counties route through state inspector
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado SB 13-126 (2013) made Colorado the first state to legally prohibit unlicensed electricians from pulling permits — every residential panel upgrade or rewire requires a CO DORA State Electrical Board card on file with the AHJ, and Master and Residential Wireman are separately tested credentials with renewal tied to continuing education in the current NEC cycle.
CO.climate_concern: Front Range diurnal swings of 50°F+ between January nights and noon, plus high-altitude solar irradiance over 8,000 feet, force panel-board ambient derating on south-facing exterior service equipment — and Continental Divide lightning concentrations above 7,000 feet drive surge protection on every service entrance and pinhole-leak inspection of buried aluminum SE-cable in expansive bentonite-clay soils that heave and shear conduit annually.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.nec_amendment_summary: Connecticut Electrical Code adopts 2020 NEC under DCP Section 20-330 with state amendments preserving E-1 unlimited contractor authority and apprentice ratio rules
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — E-1 (Unlimited) / E-2 (Limited Sub) electrical tiers
CT.permit_typical: town electrical permit required for panel upgrade, service-line replacement, and EV charging install
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut Public Act 22-25 directs the Department of Energy and Environmental Protection to fund residential battery storage and managed-charging programs, and the Connecticut Green Bank co-finances solar-plus-storage interconnection through the United Illuminating and Eversource service territories — every residential panel upgrade now factors in a 60A or 100A subpanel allowance for storage and EVSE under E-1 contractor planning.
CT.climate_concern: Aging Hartford, Bridgeport, and New Haven housing stock sits on 60A or 100A fuse panels reaching end-of-life with cloth-insulated branch wiring failing at junction-box terminations — and Long Island Sound coastal flood plain corrodes underground service-entrance conduit on Greenwich-to-New London shoreline properties, forcing copper service conductors and PVC-coated rigid conduit on tidal-exposed meter pedestals.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.nec_amendment_summary: Delaware adopts NEC under Title 24 Chapter 14 with State Board of Electrical Examiners enforcement and county-level inspection
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Professional Regulation — Board of Electrical Examiners
DE.permit_typical: county electrical permit required for new fixtures and service-line work
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware Title 24 Chapter 14 splits electrical licensure into five tiers — Master Electrician, Master Special Electrician, Limited Electrician, Journeyperson Electrician, and Apprentice — and the Limited Electrician credential is restricted to specific work categories (signs, swimming pools, PV) by Board approval rather than carrying general residential authority.
DE.climate_concern: Coastal Sussex County properties on the Inland Bays face brackish flood exposure during nor'easters, driving brass-bushed in-use covers, marine-grade THHN copper, and stainless-steel meter base hardware on every shoreline service upgrade — and the Delaware River industrial corridor's high humidity and salt-haze deposition shorten service-mast galvanic life on Wilmington-area service drops.

FL.name: Florida
FL.nec_amendment_summary: Florida Building Code 2023 (FBC 8th edition) adopts 2020 NEC with HVHZ wind-borne debris protection on Miami-Dade and Broward service equipment, Product Approval mandatory on every panel and meter base
FL.license_board_name: Florida Department of Business & Professional Regulation — Electrical Contractors Licensing Board (EC license)
FL.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required; FBC Product Approval verification on all service equipment in HVHZ counties
FL.distinctive_law: Florida is the only state where every electrical service equipment component — meter base, main breaker panel, exterior disconnect — must carry a Florida Product Approval (FL #) under FBC §1714 on Miami-Dade and Broward HVHZ projects, and the FBC requires service equipment installed within 1 mile of the coastline to use stainless-steel mounting hardware and NEMA 4X enclosures regardless of inland code defaults.
FL.climate_concern: Florida ranks first in the United States for cloud-to-ground lightning flash density at 12-15 strikes per square mile annually, driving Type 1 service-entrance SPD installation as a near-universal panel-upgrade line item — and the HVHZ wind-borne debris zone in Miami-Dade and Broward forces meter bases and exterior service equipment to pass FBC Product Approval impact testing, ruling out the standard pancake-style meter sockets common across the Southeast.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.nec_amendment_summary: Georgia adopts 2020 NEC under Georgia State Construction Industry Licensing Board with Class I Restricted (residential) and Class II Unrestricted contractor tiers
GA.license_board_name: Georgia Construction Industry Licensing Board — Division of Electrical Contractors
GA.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia splits electrical contractor licensure into Class I Restricted (residential and small commercial up to 200A service) and Class II Unrestricted (any voltage, any service) tiers — a Class I license cannot pull permits on three-phase commercial service or on any panel exceeding 200A even if the contractor is a Master Electrician, forcing many Atlanta-area contractors to maintain dual credentials.
GA.climate_concern: Atlanta's red Piedmont clay drives elevated soil resistivity that pushes grounding electrode resistance above the NEC §250.53 25-ohm trigger on a single rod, requiring chemical ground rods or supplemental Ufer/concrete-encased electrodes on most metro-area panel upgrades — and the South Georgia coastal plain faces lightning flash density above 10 strikes per square mile, comparable to Florida, with whole-house surge protection now near-universal on coastal service installs.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.nec_amendment_summary: Hawaii adopts NEC under HRS 448E with county-level adoption variances — Honolulu County on 2017 NEC, Maui and Hawaii Counties on 2014 NEC with local amendments
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Department of Commerce & Consumer Affairs — Board of Electricians and Plumbers
HI.permit_typical: county electrical permit required for panel upgrade, PV interconnection, and any service-line work
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii Act 204 (2008) requires every new single-family home to include solar water heating unless explicitly exempted by the County Building Department — and HRS 269-44 layers a renewable portfolio standard mandating 100% clean electricity by 2045, so Hawaiian Electric Net Energy Metering tariffs and battery-storage interconnection studies are now embedded in every Honolulu, Maui, and Hilo panel upgrade scope.
HI.climate_concern: Trade-wind-driven salt-haze deposition on every windward shoreline corrodes outdoor disconnect enclosures and meter sockets within 5-7 years, mandating 316 stainless steel or fiberglass NEMA 4X service equipment on Oahu's North Shore and Hilo's Hamakua Coast — and Big Island volcanic vog (sulfur dioxide) accelerates copper and aluminum service-conductor corrosion in Kona-side downwind zones.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.nec_amendment_summary: Idaho Division of Building Safety adopts current NEC cycle statewide under Idaho Code §54-1001 with separate EV charging permit requirement
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Division of Occupational and Professional Licenses — Electrical Board
ID.permit_typical: state or city electrical permit required for panel upgrade, service-line work, and EV charging
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho Code §54-1001 makes Idaho one of the few states where any electrical permit application — even by a homeowner on their own residence — must list a state-licensed Electrical Specialty Contractor or Master Electrician on the project, with the Division of Building Safety performing direct inspections in any jurisdiction without a chartered electrical inspection program.
ID.climate_concern: Snake River Plain volcanic-pumice soils provide poor grounding electrode resistance, forcing supplemental copper-clad ground rods or concrete-encased electrodes on every Boise and Twin Falls panel upgrade — and high-altitude winter air across the Sawtooth and Bitterroot ranges drives sustained sub-zero ambients that brittle PVC conduit fittings, requiring schedule 80 or rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts above 5,000 feet elevation.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.nec_amendment_summary: Illinois has no statewide electrical code — Chicago operates on its own Chicago Electrical Code (rigid metallic conduit mandatory, no NM cable), Cook County on 2017 NEC, downstate municipalities adopt independently
IL.license_board_name: No statewide license — Chicago Department of Buildings, Cook County, and downstate municipalities issue local credentials
IL.permit_typical: Chicago and Cook County electrical permits required; rigid metallic conduit on all branch circuits within Chicago city limits
IL.distinctive_law: Chicago is the only major U.S. city where romex (NM-B nonmetallic-sheathed cable) is prohibited on every residential branch circuit under the Chicago Electrical Code §18-27 — every conductor in a Chicago dwelling must be installed in EMT, IMC, or rigid metal conduit, dramatically raising labor cost on rewires versus suburban Cook County NEC-compliant work, and Chicago electricians additionally hold a Chicago Supervising Electrician license rather than a state credential.
IL.climate_concern: Lake Michigan lake-effect freeze-thaw cycling drives ground frost depth to 42 inches in Chicago and 36 inches downstate, requiring rigid PVC conduit with expansion fittings on exterior service masts and forcing buried service-entrance conduit below the frost line — and Cook County clay-loam soils with seasonal saturation provide good grounding electrode contact but pinhole-corrode buried aluminum service conductors over 30-year cycles, with copper service conductors now standard on rewires.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.nec_amendment_summary: Indiana operates on the 2008 NEC statewide under 675 IAC 17 with no statewide electrical contractor licensing — municipalities issue local credentials independently
IN.license_board_name: No statewide electrical license — Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, and Evansville issue municipal credentials
IN.permit_typical: city electrical permit required; rural counties may have no inspection program
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana is the only state with both no statewide electrical contractor licensing AND a deliberately frozen NEC adoption — 675 IAC 17 still references the 2008 National Electrical Code, meaning AFCI and tamper-resistant receptacle requirements that became standard elsewhere in 2014–2017 are not enforced statewide and only apply where a municipality (Indianapolis, Fort Wayne) has independently adopted a more recent cycle.
IN.climate_concern: The Wabash and White River valleys face seasonal flood inundation that drowns ground-mounted meter pedestals annually, forcing meter-base elevation above the 100-year flood elevation on FEMA-mapped properties — and Hoosier National Forest karst terrain in Orange and Crawford counties provides wildly variable grounding electrode resistance, requiring chemical ground rods or alternate Ufer electrodes on rural panel upgrades.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.nec_amendment_summary: Iowa adopts 2020 NEC under Iowa Code §103.6 with Iowa Electrical Examining Board oversight and unified statewide enforcement
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Department of Inspections and Appeals — Electrical Examining Board
IA.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Code §103.6 makes the Iowa Electrical Examining Board the exclusive licensing authority for the entire state — there are no carve-outs for cities or counties to issue independent electrician credentials, unlike neighboring Illinois and Missouri — and the Board issues seven distinct license classes from Apprentice through Class A Master Electrician with renewal tied to NEC cycle continuing education.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa's tornado alley exposure produces straight-line wind events that snap service masts on overhead service drops every spring — utility-mast bracing and pinned weatherheads are increasingly written into Quad Cities and Des Moines panel upgrades — and frost depth to 42 inches in northern Iowa drives buried service-entrance conduit below the frost line plus expansion fittings on exterior masts.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.nec_amendment_summary: Kansas has no statewide electrical contractor licensing — Wichita, Kansas City, Topeka, and Overland Park issue local credentials and adopt NEC cycles independently
KS.license_board_name: No statewide electrical license — Wichita Office of Central Inspection, Unified Government of Wyandotte County, and Topeka Development Services issue municipal credentials
KS.permit_typical: city electrical permit required for panel upgrade in incorporated municipalities; no state inspection program for unincorporated areas
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas remains one of fewer than ten states with no statewide electrical contractor licensing — every credential is municipal, with Wichita, Kansas City Kansas, Overland Park, and Topeka each running independent journeyman and master examinations — and rural unincorporated counties may have no inspection program at all, meaning a homeowner panel upgrade in unincorporated Sumner County faces no NEC-compliance verification.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas sits inside the central United States hail and straight-line wind corridor with annual tornado exposure across the entire state — service masts and overhead drops on Wichita-to-Topeka properties face 70-100 mph downbursts that bend the standard 1.25-inch RMC mast, and reinforced service-entrance bracing plus copper service conductors are now standard on rewires.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.nec_amendment_summary: Kentucky adopts 2017 NEC under KRS 227 with Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction enforcement and Master/Journeyman/Electrical Contractor tiers
KY.license_board_name: Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction — Division of Plumbing and Electrical
KY.permit_typical: state or city electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky KRS 227.480 establishes Master Electrician, Electrical Contractor, and Journeyman as three distinct credentials, but unique to Kentucky an Electrical Contractor license is a business-entity credential separate from the Master Electrician personal credential — meaning a holder must maintain both to legally bid and execute work on a residential panel upgrade.
KY.climate_concern: Eastern Kentucky's coal-country reclaimed mine subsidence creates unpredictable ground settlement that shears buried service-entrance conduit annually on properties built over abandoned mine works — and the Ohio River valley corridor's high-humidity summer thunderstorm activity drives lightning flash density above the national average, with whole-house surge protection now near-universal on Louisville and Lexington panel upgrades.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.nec_amendment_summary: Louisiana State Uniform Construction Code adopts 2017 NEC with hurricane wind-design overlays on Gulf Coast parishes
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors — Electrical Work classification
LA.permit_typical: parish electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors RS 37:2150 issues the Electrical Work classification to firms whose project value exceeds $7,500, while the State Fire Marshal Office of State Fire Prevention regulates electrical inspection in parishes without local programs — and in the New Orleans flood-protection corridor every meter base must be elevated above the Base Flood Elevation under FEMA NFIP rules to maintain insurance discount eligibility.
LA.climate_concern: Louisiana ties Florida for cloud-to-ground lightning flash density across the Gulf Coast parishes, and post-Katrina flood-elevation rules now require meter pedestals to sit above the FEMA Base Flood Elevation in every coastal parish — combined with annual hurricane-season service-mast wind loading, panel upgrades in Orleans, Jefferson, and Lafourche parishes routinely include weatherhead reinforcement and Type 1 service-entrance surge protection as default scope.

ME.name: Maine
ME.nec_amendment_summary: Maine Electricians' Examining Board adopts 2017 NEC under 32 MRS §1101 with Master, Journeyman, and Limited credentials and statewide enforcement
ME.license_board_name: Maine Department of Professional and Financial Regulation — Electricians' Examining Board
ME.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required where adopted; state inspection in unincorporated areas
ME.distinctive_law: Maine 32 MRS §1101 is one of the most tiered electrician licensing statutes in the country with five separate credentials — Master Electrician, Limited Electrician (specific scope categories), Journeyman Electrician, Journeyman in Training, and Helper Electrician — each requiring different combinations of work hours, classroom education, and Examining Board oral defense before issuance.
ME.climate_concern: Maine's coastal salt-haze on the Down East and Mid-Coast corridors corrodes outdoor service equipment on every shoreline property, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets — and frost depth to 60 inches in Aroostook County requires service-entrance conduit buried below the frost line with rigid PVC schedule 80 or galvanized RMC, plus expansion fittings on exterior service masts to absorb seasonal differential.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.nec_amendment_summary: Maryland adopts 2020 NEC under State Board of Master Electricians with county-level inspection programs and Baltimore City separate enforcement
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Department of Labor — State Board of Master Electricians
MD.permit_typical: county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work; Baltimore City separate inspection
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland's State Board of Master Electricians issues only the Master Electrician credential — Journeyman and Apprentice tiers are issued at the county level, creating a parallel state-and-county credentialing system unique to Maryland where a contractor must hold both the state Master credential and the relevant county Journeyman cards to staff a residential project legally.
MD.climate_concern: Chesapeake Bay coastal counties from St. Mary's to Cecil face brackish flood inundation during nor'easters that drowns ground-mounted meter pedestals, forcing meter-base elevation under FEMA NFIP rules — and the Baltimore-Washington urban corridor's mature tree canopy drives utility line-down events during winter ice storms that severely overload service drops, with reinforced weatherheads and copper service conductors increasingly common on rewires.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.nec_amendment_summary: Massachusetts Electrical Code (527 CMR 12) adopts 2023 NEC with state amendments preserving Mass-specific apprentice ratios, AFCI on dwelling unit branch circuits, and arc-fault TRs
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Board of State Examiners of Electricians
MA.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work; CSL filing required separately
MA.distinctive_law: Massachusetts 527 CMR 12 is one of the most heavily amended NEC adoptions in the country — the Massachusetts Electrical Code retains state-specific apprentice-to-journeyman ratios, mandatory continuing-education requirements every cycle, and prohibition of multi-wire branch circuits in dwelling unit AFCI installations — and Master Electrician (A), Journeyman (B), and Master Restricted (C) are three separately tested Board credentials.
MA.climate_concern: Cape Cod and South Shore coastal salt-haze deposition shortens galvanized service-mast life to 7-10 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and aging Boston, Worcester, and Springfield triple-decker housing stock with knob-and-tube branch wiring forces complete rewire scope on most pre-1950 panel upgrades to satisfy 527 CMR 12 §250 grounding electrode replacement requirements.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.nec_amendment_summary: Michigan adopts 2017 NEC under PA 217 of 1956 (Skilled Trades Regulation Act) with Bureau of Construction Codes enforcement and three-tier licensing
MI.license_board_name: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs — Bureau of Construction Codes (Electrical Licensing Section)
MI.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan Public Act 217 of 1956 (the Skilled Trades Regulation Act) issues Master Electrician, Journeyman, Sign Specialist, Fire Alarm Specialist, and Electrical Contractor as five separately tested credentials — and unique to Michigan, the Electrical Contractor license is a business-entity credential that requires a designated Master Electrician on file, with the Master credential separately renewable on an individual basis.
MI.climate_concern: Upper Peninsula winter ambient sustains -20°F to -40°F across Houghton, Marquette, and Sault Ste. Marie service drops, brittling PVC conduit fittings and forcing rigid metal conduit on exterior service masts above 45° latitude — and Lake Michigan and Lake Superior lake-effect freeze-thaw drives ground frost depth to 60 inches in the U.P., requiring service-entrance conduit buried below the frost line with rigid schedule 80 PVC.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.nec_amendment_summary: Minnesota adopts 2020 NEC under MN Statutes 326B.31 with State Board of Electricity unified enforcement and Master/Class A/B credentialing
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota Department of Labor & Industry — State Board of Electricity
MN.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statutes 326B.31 makes the State Board of Electricity the unified licensing authority across all 87 counties — and unique to Minnesota, the Class A Master Electrician credential (the highest tier) requires not only the standard Master examination but additional testing on the Minnesota State Building Code amendments, with a Class B Master limited to single-family residential work.
MN.climate_concern: Northern Minnesota's frost depth reaches 80 inches in International Falls and the Iron Range, requiring service-entrance conduit buried below the frost line with rigid metallic conduit and schedule 80 PVC fittings — and sustained sub-zero winter ambients across the Boundary Waters drive copper service conductors over aluminum on every rewire to avoid the cold-flow creep that plagues aluminum lugs in panel terminations at extreme temperatures.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.nec_amendment_summary: Mississippi adopts 2014 NEC under MS State Board of Contractors with classification 'EE' Electrical for projects over $50,000 only — sub-$50k residential work has no statewide license requirement
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi State Board of Contractors — EE Electrical (only for projects >$50,000)
MS.permit_typical: city electrical permit required in incorporated municipalities; rural counties may have no inspection program
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi State Board of Contractors only requires the EE Electrical contractor classification on residential projects with total contract value above $50,000 — meaning the overwhelming majority of single-family panel upgrades, rewires, and service-line replacements proceed under municipal credentials only, with no state oversight in unincorporated counties without chartered inspection programs.
MS.climate_concern: The Mississippi Gulf Coast and Pine Belt sit inside the highest cloud-to-ground lightning flash density region in the United States outside Florida, driving Type 1 service-entrance SPD as a near-universal panel upgrade line item — and post-Katrina flood-elevation rules require meter pedestals to sit above the FEMA Base Flood Elevation in Hancock, Harrison, and Jackson counties to maintain NFIP insurance eligibility.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.nec_amendment_summary: Missouri has no statewide electrical contractor licensing — Kansas City, St. Louis, Springfield, and Columbia each issue local credentials and adopt NEC cycles independently
MO.license_board_name: No statewide electrical license — Kansas City and St. Louis issue municipal credentials
MO.permit_typical: city electrical permit required in incorporated municipalities; rural counties may have no inspection program
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri remains one of fewer than ten states with no statewide electrical licensing program — Kansas City and St. Louis run reciprocal credentialing for journeymen, but rural Boone, Cole, and Greene counties may issue homeowner-only permits with no contractor verification, and unincorporated areas frequently have no inspection program at all.
MO.climate_concern: The Missouri River and Mississippi River bottomland exposes thousands of single-family parcels to FEMA-mapped 100-year flood elevation, forcing meter pedestals above BFE on St. Louis County, Jefferson County, and Boone County riverfront properties — and Kansas City's freeze-thaw cycling on expansive Boone-series clay drives ground heave that shears buried service-entrance PVC conduit annually on northern Missouri panel upgrades.

MT.name: Montana
MT.nec_amendment_summary: Montana adopts 2020 NEC under Title 37 with State Electrical Board enforcement and Master/Journeyman/Residential tier credentialing
MT.license_board_name: Montana Board of Plumbers, Electricians, and Mechanical Contractors — State Electrical Board
MT.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
MT.distinctive_law: Montana Title 37 Chapter 68 establishes Master Electrician, Journeyman, and Residential as three Board-issued credentials — and unique to Montana, the Residential Electrician license is a separately tested credential restricted to one- and two-family dwellings up to 200A service, allowing a more accessible entry-level residential pathway than the full Journeyman exam.
MT.climate_concern: Continental winter ambients across Glacier, Flathead, and Lewis & Clark counties sustain -30°F to -50°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and Big Sky Country wildfire-urban interface (WUI) overlay zones now require buried service drops on new construction in Park, Madison, and Gallatin counties to eliminate overhead service drops as wildfire ignition sources.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.nec_amendment_summary: Nebraska adopts 2023 NEC under State Electrical Division with statewide enforcement through the Nebraska Energy Office
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska State Electrical Division — State Electrical Board
NE.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska is one of the few states where the State Electrical Division operates as a freestanding agency rather than under a broader Department of Labor or Licensing umbrella — the Division directly inspects every state-jurisdictional installation and maintains separate credentials for Class A Contractor, Class B Contractor (residential <200A), Master Electrician, and Journeyman across the state.
NE.climate_concern: Nebraska sits inside the central Plains hail and tornado corridor with annual exposure across the entire state — Omaha and Lincoln service masts face 70-100 mph downbursts that bend the standard 1.25-inch RMC mast, and reinforced weatherheads with copper service conductors are now standard on Lincoln-Omaha panel upgrades to survive recurring derecho-driven service drop failures.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.nec_amendment_summary: Nevada adopts 2020 NEC under Nevada State Contractors Board (NSCB) with C-2 Electrical classification and county-level inspection
NV.license_board_name: Nevada State Contractors Board — C-2 Electrical classification
NV.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade, service-line work, and PV interconnection
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada is the only state where the Public Utilities Commission limits NV Energy Net Energy Metering compensation at 75% of the retail rate under Tier 4 of the 2017 settlement — driving battery-storage interconnection on every Las Vegas and Reno residential PV panel upgrade to capture the full retail value of self-consumption rather than the discounted export rate.
NV.climate_concern: Las Vegas Valley summer attic ambients reach 165°F, derating THWN-2 conductors and forcing copper rather than aluminum on PV homerun pulls and combiner-box runs — and Mojave Desert sand and silica deposition corrodes aluminum meter sockets and outdoor disconnect knockouts within 5-7 years, mandating gasketed in-use covers and NEMA 3R enclosures on every exterior receptacle and service-entrance disconnect.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.nec_amendment_summary: New Hampshire adopts 2020 NEC under RSA 319-C with the Electricians' Board issuing Master, Journeyman, and Apprentice credentials statewide
NH.license_board_name: New Hampshire Office of Professional Licensure and Certification — Electricians' Board
NH.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire RSA 319-C is one of the older standalone electrician licensing statutes in the country, dating to the 1947 Electricians' Board establishment — and unique to New Hampshire, the Master Electrician credential requires four years of journeyman-level work experience plus passage of both NEC and New Hampshire-specific code amendments before issuance, with continuing education tied to each NEC adoption cycle.
NH.climate_concern: White Mountain elevation winter ambients across Coos and Grafton counties sustain -25°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and frost depth to 60 inches in the North Country drives buried service-entrance conduit below the frost line, with seasonal Connecticut River valley flooding adding meter-base elevation requirements on FEMA-mapped riverfront parcels.

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.nec_amendment_summary: New Jersey adopts 2020 NEC with state amendments under N.J.A.C. 13:31 — Bureau of Electrical Subcode regulates apprentices, journeymen, and electrical contractors statewide
NJ.license_board_name: New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs — Board of Examiners of Electrical Contractors
NJ.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work; UCC subcode official inspection
NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey N.J.A.C. 13:31 makes the Electrical Contractor business license a corporate-entity credential separate from the individual Qualifier credential — the Qualifier (typically a Master Electrician) must be on the contractor's payroll for the license to remain active, and a contractor losing its Qualifier has 90 days to replace before license suspension, unique among Mid-Atlantic states.
NJ.climate_concern: New Jersey Shore coastal salt-haze deposition from Cape May to Sandy Hook corrodes outdoor service equipment within 5-7 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and the Pine Barrens' high water table drowns ground-mounted meter pedestals during nor'easter rainfall events, forcing meter-base elevation above the local water table on Burlington and Ocean county properties.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.nec_amendment_summary: New Mexico adopts 2017 NEC under Construction Industries Division with EE-98 (Master Electrician) and ES-1 (Electrical Specialty) classifications
NM.license_board_name: New Mexico Regulation and Licensing Department — Construction Industries Division (Electrical)
NM.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
NM.distinctive_law: New Mexico Construction Industries Division issues EE-98 (Master Electrician) and EJ-98 (Journeyman Electrician) as separately tested classifications, with EE-98 holders authorized to operate as Qualifying Parties for any electrical contracting business — and unique to New Mexico, the Electrical Bureau directly inspects every state-jurisdictional install rather than delegating to municipal inspectors in unincorporated counties.
NM.climate_concern: High-desert solar irradiance across the Albuquerque-to-Las Cruces corridor at 5,000-7,000 feet elevation drives PV panel-upgrade demand, with Santa Fe and Taos winter ambients reaching -10°F that brittle PVC fittings on exterior service masts — and gypsum-rich soils across the Permian Basin provide poor grounding electrode resistance, forcing chemical ground rods or supplemental concrete-encased Ufer electrodes on most southern New Mexico panel upgrades.

NY.name: New York
NY.nec_amendment_summary: New York has no statewide electrical contractor license — NYC, Yonkers, Long Island, and Buffalo issue independent municipal credentials with NYC operating its own Electrical Code (NYC EC) parallel to NEC
NY.license_board_name: No statewide electrical license — NYC Department of Buildings, Westchester, Nassau, and Suffolk issue municipal credentials
NY.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required; NYC requires Licensed Master Electrician on every job
NY.distinctive_law: New York City operates its own Electrical Code (the NYC EC, currently 2011 cycle with substantial 2008 NEC overlay) entirely parallel to and distinct from the National Electrical Code used in the rest of the state — every NYC panel upgrade requires a Licensed Master Electrician's seal on the filing, with the LME credential issued by the NYC Department of Buildings rather than any state authority, and rigid metallic conduit is mandatory on most circuits.
NY.climate_concern: NYC and Long Island coastal storm surge during Sandy-class events flooded thousands of meter pedestals and service equipment — FEMA NFIP elevation-above-BFE rules now drive meter-base elevation on every coastal Brooklyn, Queens, and Long Island upgrade, and Adirondack winter ambients in St. Lawrence and Franklin counties brittle PVC fittings, requiring rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts above 44° latitude.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.nec_amendment_summary: North Carolina adopts 2017 NEC with NC State Building Code amendments under State Board of Examiners of Electrical Contractors
NC.license_board_name: North Carolina State Board of Examiners of Electrical Contractors
NC.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
NC.distinctive_law: North Carolina General Statutes 87-43 establishes Limited (under $50k single-family work), Intermediate ($50k-$130k), and Unlimited (any size) as three separately tested electrical contractor classifications — and unique to NC, the Board issues Single-Family Dwelling Specialty (SP-SFD), Residential Dwelling (SP-RD), Plate Glass Specialty, and other restricted classifications letting smaller contractors specialize within scope rather than testing for the full Unlimited credential.
NC.climate_concern: Outer Banks and Pamlico Sound coastal salt-haze deposition corrodes outdoor service equipment within 5-7 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and the eastern Coastal Plain faces hurricane wind exposure with category-rated service-mast bracing increasingly written into Wilmington, New Bern, and Outer Banks panel upgrades.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.nec_amendment_summary: North Dakota adopts 2020 NEC under State Electrical Board with statewide enforcement and Master/Journeyman/Class B credentialing
ND.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
ND.distinctive_law: North Dakota's State Electrical Board is one of the few state agencies that directly performs every electrical inspection in unincorporated counties — Board inspectors cover the entire 70,000-square-mile state, with municipalities like Fargo, Bismarck, and Grand Forks running independent inspection programs only within incorporated city limits, and the Class B Master credential is restricted to single-family residential up to 200A service.
ND.climate_concern: Northern Plains winter ambients across Williston, Minot, and Grand Forks sustain -30°F to -50°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and frost depth to 80 inches in the Red River Valley requires service-entrance conduit buried below the frost line with rigid schedule 80 PVC and expansion fittings to absorb seasonal differential.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.nec_amendment_summary: Ohio adopts 2017 NEC under OBOC residential code with Construction Industry Licensing Board issuing statewide Electrical Contractor credentials for projects above $25k
OH.license_board_name: Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board — Electrical Contractor
OH.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
OH.distinctive_law: Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board issues the statewide Electrical Contractor credential only for projects with total value above $25,000 — single-family residential panel upgrades and rewires under that threshold proceed under municipal credentials only, with cities like Cincinnati, Columbus, and Cleveland running independent journeyman and master examination programs that don't reciprocate with the state credential.
OH.climate_concern: Lake Erie shoreline counties from Lucas to Ashtabula face lake-effect winter freeze-thaw cycling that drives ground frost depth to 42 inches and shears buried service-entrance PVC conduit annually — and the Ohio River industrial corridor's high humidity and coal-fired plant downwind sulfate deposition shorten outdoor service-mast galvanic life on Cincinnati and Steubenville-area service drops.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.nec_amendment_summary: Oklahoma adopts 2017 NEC under Construction Industries Board with statewide enforcement and Electrical Contractor / Journeyman / Apprentice credentialing
OK.license_board_name: Oklahoma Construction Industries Board — Electrical License
OK.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
OK.distinctive_law: Oklahoma Construction Industries Board issues Electrical Contractor as a contracting-firm credential, separately from the personal Master and Journeyman credentials — and a Contractor credential requires both an active Master Electrician on staff AND a $5,000 surety bond on file, with bond suspension immediately voiding the contracting authority statewide.
OK.climate_concern: Oklahoma sits at the heart of Tornado Alley with annual EF2-EF5 exposure across the entire state, and Moore-Norman-Oklahoma City corridor service masts face downbursts that bend standard 1.25-inch RMC masts every spring — reinforced weatherheads, copper service conductors, and underground service drops are increasingly written into Oklahoma City and Tulsa panel upgrades to survive recurring derecho and tornado damage.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.nec_amendment_summary: Oregon adopts 2023 NEC under OAR 918 with Building Codes Division enforcement and General Supervising Electrician (GS) / General Journeyman (J) / Limited Energy (LEA/LEB) tiered licensing
OR.license_board_name: Oregon Building Codes Division — Electrical & Elevator Board
OR.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
OR.distinctive_law: Oregon BCD issues seven distinct electrical licenses — General Supervising Electrician, General Journeyman, Limited Energy A (high-voltage signs), Limited Energy B (low-voltage), Limited Maintenance Electrician, Plant Electrician, and Restricted Energy Technician — making Oregon the most tiered-credential state in the country and forcing PV-plus-storage installers to often hold both the GS and LEB credentials simultaneously.
OR.climate_concern: Cascadia subduction zone seismic exposure across the I-5 corridor from Portland to Eugene drives flexible-conduit and seismic-anchorage requirements on every meter base, panel, and load-side connection — and Pacific Northwest coastal salt-haze across Tillamook, Lincoln, and Coos counties corrodes outdoor service equipment within 7-10 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.nec_amendment_summary: Pennsylvania has no statewide electrical contractor licensing — Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, Allentown, and Lancaster issue independent municipal credentials and adopt NEC cycles independently
PA.license_board_name: No statewide electrical license — Philadelphia L&I, Pittsburgh BBI, and other cities issue municipal credentials
PA.permit_typical: city or municipal electrical permit required; rural townships may have no inspection program
PA.distinctive_law: Pennsylvania remains one of fewer than ten states with no statewide electrical contractor licensing, despite repeated General Assembly bills proposing one — Philadelphia L&I issues its own four-tier Master Electrician credential under City Code §9-1004, Pittsburgh BBI issues a separate Master Electrician credential, and the two cities do not reciprocate, forcing PA-wide contractors to maintain multiple municipal credentials in parallel.
PA.climate_concern: Pennsylvania's Allegheny Plateau and Pocono regions face winter ambients reaching -15°F that brittle PVC fittings on exterior service masts — and the Marcellus Shale extraction corridor in Bradford, Susquehanna, and Tioga counties has produced ground-subsidence claims that shear buried service-entrance conduit on properties above active underground workings, with utility-mast bracing increasingly common on rewires.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.nec_amendment_summary: Rhode Island adopts 2020 NEC under RIGL 5-6 with Department of Labor & Training Electrical Section issuing License A (Unlimited Contractor), License B (Limited Contractor), and Journeyperson credentials
RI.license_board_name: Rhode Island Department of Labor & Training — Division of Workforce Regulation & Safety (Electrical)
RI.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
RI.distinctive_law: Rhode Island RIGL 5-6 issues License A (Unlimited Contractor) and License B (Limited Contractor, residential <200A) as two business-entity electrical credentials separate from the personal Journeyperson tier — and unique to Rhode Island, the License A credential additionally requires three Journeyperson Electricians on the contractor's payroll at all times to maintain active status, with payroll falling below threshold triggering immediate license suspension.
RI.climate_concern: Narragansett Bay coastal salt-haze deposition across Newport, Bristol, and Washington counties corrodes outdoor service equipment within 5-7 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and aging Providence and Pawtucket housing stock with knob-and-tube branch wiring forces complete rewire scope on most pre-1940 panel upgrades to satisfy state grounding electrode replacement requirements.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.nec_amendment_summary: South Carolina adopts 2020 NEC under SC LLR — Residential Builders Commission with Specialty Contractor SP-RBE classification for residential electrical
SC.license_board_name: South Carolina Department of Labor, Licensing and Regulation — Residential Builders Commission (SP-RBE Electrical Specialty)
SC.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
SC.distinctive_law: South Carolina SC Code §40-59 places residential electrical work under the Residential Builders Commission rather than a freestanding electrical board — Residential Specialty Contractor SP-RBE (Electrical) is the credential required to bid and execute single-family panel upgrades and rewires, and the Commission also requires the Residential Builder's General Contractor credential on any project where electrical is bundled with structural work.
SC.climate_concern: Charleston, Hilton Head, and Myrtle Beach Atlantic salt-haze deposition corrodes outdoor service equipment within 5-7 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and the Lowcountry hurricane wind exposure across Beaufort, Charleston, and Horry counties drives reinforced weatherheads and category-rated service-mast bracing on every coastal panel upgrade.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.nec_amendment_summary: South Dakota adopts 2023 NEC under State Electrical Commission with statewide enforcement and Class A/Class B/Journeyman credentialing
SD.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
SD.distinctive_law: South Dakota's State Electrical Commission is one of the smallest state agencies in the country (under 30 inspectors statewide) — the Commission directly inspects every state-jurisdictional installation, with no county-level inspection programs, and the Class B Master credential is restricted to single-family residential up to 200A service while Class A authorizes any voltage and any service size.
SD.climate_concern: Northern Plains winter ambients across Pierre, Aberdeen, and Rapid City sustain -30°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and Black Hills granite outcrops provide poor grounding electrode contact, requiring chemical ground rods or supplemental concrete-encased Ufer electrodes on most western South Dakota panel upgrades.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.nec_amendment_summary: Tennessee adopts 2017 NEC under State Fire Marshal — Limited Licensed Electrician (LLE) credential required for projects under $25k, full Electrical Contractor for higher
TN.license_board_name: Tennessee Department of Commerce & Insurance — Board for Licensing Contractors (Electrical)
TN.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
TN.distinctive_law: Tennessee Department of Commerce & Insurance issues the Limited Licensed Electrician (LLE) credential for residential and small-commercial projects under $25,000 — distinct from the full Electrical Contractor classification required on larger projects — and the LLE credential is administered by the State Fire Marshal's Office through written and practical examinations rather than the Board for Licensing Contractors.
TN.climate_concern: Tennessee Valley Authority service territory across the eastern and central state faces high cloud-to-ground lightning flash density driven by summer cumulonimbus activity — and Memphis-area Mississippi River bottomland exposes thousands of single-family parcels to FEMA-mapped flood elevation, forcing meter pedestals above BFE on Shelby County riverfront properties to maintain NFIP insurance eligibility.

TX.name: Texas
TX.nec_amendment_summary: Texas adopts 2020 NEC under Texas Department of Licensing & Regulation with state-amendment overlays — Master Electrician (ME), Journeyman Electrician (JE), and Maintenance Electrician (MEC) tiers
TX.license_board_name: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation — Electrical Contractors and Electricians
TX.permit_typical: city or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
TX.distinctive_law: Texas TDLR Electrical Safety and Licensing Act (Texas Occupations Code Chapter 1305) makes Texas one of the few states with both a unified statewide Master and Journeyman electrician credential AND a separate Electrical Contractor business-entity credential — and unique to Texas, the Maintenance Electrician (MEC) credential is restricted to in-house maintenance work for a single industrial owner, not licensed for public contracting.
TX.climate_concern: Winter Storm Uri (2021) collapsed the ERCOT grid for 4+ days and exposed Texas-wide service-equipment vulnerability to extreme cold — battery-storage and standby-generator interconnection on service panels has tripled in scope on Houston, Dallas, Austin, and San Antonio panel upgrades since 2021, and Hill Country and West Texas service drops face annual derecho-driven downbursts that bend standard 1.25-inch RMC service masts.

UT.name: Utah
UT.nec_amendment_summary: Utah adopts 2020 NEC under Utah Code Title 58 Chapter 55 with Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing enforcement and Master/Journeyman/Residential tier credentialing
UT.license_board_name: Utah Department of Commerce — Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing (Electrical)
UT.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
UT.distinctive_law: Utah DOPL issues a Residential Master Electrician credential as a separately tested classification distinct from the full Master Electrician — the Residential Master is restricted to one- and two-family dwellings up to 200A service, allowing a more accessible entry-level pathway than the Unlimited Master exam, with both credentials requiring 8,000 hours of journeyman experience before sitting for examination.
UT.climate_concern: Wasatch Front high-altitude winter inversion drives sustained sub-zero ambients across Salt Lake, Davis, and Utah counties — brittling PVC conduit fittings and forcing rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts above 5,000 feet elevation — and Bonneville Salt Flats and Great Salt Lake brine deposition corrodes outdoor service equipment on western Utah parcels within 7-10 years.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.nec_amendment_summary: Vermont adopts 2017 NEC under VT Statutes Annotated Title 26 Chapter 15 with Office of Professional Regulation issuing Master, Type S Master, and Journeyman credentials
VT.license_board_name: Vermont Office of Professional Regulation — Electricians
VT.permit_typical: municipal electrical permit required where adopted; state inspection in unincorporated towns
VT.distinctive_law: Vermont Office of Professional Regulation issues Master Electrician, Type S Master Electrician (single-family dwelling restricted), Journeyman, Type J Journeyman (single-family dwelling restricted), and Apprentice as five separately tested credentials — making Vermont one of the most tiered residential-electrical credentialing systems in New England, with Type S and Type J restricted to one- and two-family dwellings.
VT.climate_concern: Green Mountain elevation winter ambients across Caledonia, Essex, and Orleans counties sustain -25°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and the Lake Champlain valley's seasonal flooding adds meter-base elevation requirements on FEMA-mapped Burlington and Franklin county lakefront parcels.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.nec_amendment_summary: Virginia adopts 2020 NEC under VA USBC with DPOR Electrical contractor credentialing — Class A (any size), Class B ($10k-$120k), Class C (under $10k) tiers
VA.license_board_name: Virginia Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation — Board for Contractors (Electrical)
VA.permit_typical: city or county electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
VA.distinctive_law: Virginia DPOR issues Class A (any project size), Class B ($10,000-$120,000), and Class C (under $10,000) as three contracting tiers — and unique to Virginia, the Master Electrician credential is a personal certification separate from the contractor credential, with the contractor required to designate a Master Electrician as the Designated Employee on every panel upgrade or service-line project.
VA.climate_concern: Chesapeake Bay coastal salt-haze deposition across Hampton Roads, Virginia Beach, and the Eastern Shore corrodes outdoor service equipment within 5-7 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware and NEMA 4X meter sockets on shoreline upgrades — and Blue Ridge winter ice storms on the western Virginia counties drive overhead service-drop failures with reinforced weatherheads and copper service conductors increasingly written into rewires.

WA.name: Washington
WA.nec_amendment_summary: Washington adopts 2020 NEC under WAC 296-46B with L&I Electrical Section issuing Administrator (01-04), Master (01-04), Journeyman, and Specialty (06/07/etc.) credentials
WA.license_board_name: Washington State Department of Labor & Industries — Electrical Section
WA.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
WA.distinctive_law: Washington L&I issues an Administrator credential at four levels (01 General, 02 Residential, 03 Pump & Irrigation, 04 Sign) plus separate Master Electrician credentials at the same four levels — making Washington the most heavily tiered electrical-licensing state in the country, with separate testing required for each scope category and the Administrator credential mandatory for every contractor business entity statewide.
WA.climate_concern: Cascadia subduction zone seismic exposure across the I-5 corridor from Bellingham to Vancouver drives flexible-conduit and seismic-anchorage requirements on every meter base, panel, and load-side connection — and Puget Sound coastal salt-haze plus Olympic Peninsula sustained rainfall corrode outdoor service equipment within 7-10 years, mandating stainless mounting hardware on shoreline panel upgrades.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.nec_amendment_summary: West Virginia adopts 2017 NEC under State Fire Marshal with Electrician License — Class A (Master), Class B (Journeyman), Class C (Apprentice) tier credentialing statewide
WV.license_board_name: West Virginia State Fire Marshal — Electrician Licensing Section
WV.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
WV.distinctive_law: West Virginia is one of the few states where the State Fire Marshal — rather than a freestanding electrical board or contractor licensing board — issues all electrical credentials under the WV Code §29-3D Electrician Licensing Act, with Class A (Master), Class B (Journeyman), Class C (Apprentice), and a separate Sign Specialist credential, and the Fire Marshal's office performs every electrical inspection in unincorporated counties.
WV.climate_concern: Appalachian coal-country reclaimed mine subsidence creates unpredictable ground settlement that shears buried service-entrance conduit annually on properties built over abandoned mine works — and Allegheny Plateau winter ice storms across Hampshire, Hardy, and Pendleton counties drive overhead service-drop failures with reinforced weatherheads now standard on western West Virginia panel upgrades.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.nec_amendment_summary: Wisconsin adopts 2020 NEC under SPS 316 (Wisconsin Electrical Code) with Department of Safety and Professional Services issuing Master, Journeyman, and Industrial Electrician credentials
WI.license_board_name: Wisconsin Department of Safety and Professional Services — Electrical License
WI.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin SPS 316 (the Wisconsin Electrical Code) makes substantial state-specific amendments to the NEC including different AFCI applicability rules and Master Electrician (residential and commercial scope), Journeyman (residential, industrial, and commercial scope), Industrial Journeyman, and Apprentice as separately tested credentials — and unique to Wisconsin, the Industrial Electrician credential is restricted to in-house facility work and prohibited from public contracting.
WI.climate_concern: Northern Wisconsin winter ambients across Vilas, Iron, and Bayfield counties sustain -30°F to -40°F readings that brittle PVC conduit fittings and force rigid metallic conduit on exterior service masts — and Lake Michigan and Lake Superior lake-effect freeze-thaw drives ground frost depth to 60 inches in the North Woods, requiring service-entrance conduit buried below the frost line.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.nec_amendment_summary: Wyoming adopts 2020 NEC under State Electrical Board with statewide enforcement and Master/Journeyman/Residential Master credentialing
WY.license_board_name: Wyoming Department of Workforce Services — State Electrical Board
WY.permit_typical: state or municipal electrical permit required for panel upgrade and any service work
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming State Electrical Board issues a Residential Master Electrician credential as a separately tested classification distinct from the full Master Electrician — the Residential Master is restricted to one- and two-family dwellings up to 320A service, with both credentials requiring 8,000 hours of journeyman experience and Wyoming-specific exam coverage including high-altitude derating amendments.
WY.climate_concern: Wyoming high-plains winter ambients across Sheridan, Casper, and Laramie sustain -25°F readings with sustained 50-mph chinook winds that bend service masts annually — reinforced weatherheads, copper service conductors, and underground service drops are increasingly written into Cheyenne and Casper panel upgrades, and Yellowstone-area high-altitude service installs require ambient derating on conductor sizing under NEC 310.15(B).

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.nec_amendment_summary: DC adopts 2017 NEC under DCMR Title 12 with DCRA Electrical Section issuing Master Electrician and Journeyman credentials specific to the District
DC.license_board_name: Department of Buildings (DOB) — Electrical Trade Licensing
DC.permit_typical: DC electrical permit required for panel upgrade and service-line work; DOB inspection
DC.distinctive_law: DC DCMR Title 12 Section 100.2 issues Master Electrician and Journeyman Electrician credentials specific to the District — unlike Virginia or Maryland reciprocity, DC does not accept either Maryland or Virginia electrician credentials directly, requiring separate DC examination and DOB filing for any contractor wishing to bid panel upgrades or service work within District boundaries.
DC.climate_concern: DC's dense Federal-period housing stock with knob-and-tube branch wiring forces complete rewire scope on most pre-1940 row-house panel upgrades to satisfy DCMR 12 grounding electrode replacement requirements — and the Anacostia and Potomac flood-elevation overlays drive meter-base elevation above FEMA Base Flood Elevation on Southwest waterfront and Anacostia-area properties.
